coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in colorimetry/fluorimetry-based dual-modal sensing technologies
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yaofeng
Huang, Xiaolin
Hu, Xinyu
Tong, Weipeng
Leng, Yuankui
Xiong, Yonghua
coredata.dc:description: Tailored to the increasing demands for sensing technologies, the fabrication of dual-modal sensing technologies through combining two signal transduction channels into one method has been proposed and drawn considerable attention. The integration of two sensing signals not only promotes the analytical efficiency with reduced assumption, but also improves the analytical performances with enlarged detection linear range, enhanced accuracy, and boosted application flexibility. The two top-rated output signals for developing dual-modal sensors are colorimetric and fluorescent signals because of their outstanding merits for point of care applications and real-time sensitive sensing. Given the rapid development of material chemistry and nanotechnology, the recent decade has witnessed great advance in colorimetric/fluorimetric signal based dual-modal sensing technologies. The new sensing strategy leads to a broad avenue for various applications in disease diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety because of the complementary and synergistic effects of the two output signals. In this state-of-the-art review, we comprehensively summarize different types of colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensing methods by highlighting representative research in the last 5 years, digging into their sensing methodologies, particularly the working principles of the signal transduction systems. Then, the challenges and future prospects for boosting further development of this research field are discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-modal sensing technologies
Colorimetric
Fluorimetric
Disease diagnosis
Environmental monitoring
Food safety

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tailored to the increasing demands for sensing technologies, the fabrication of dual-modal sensing technologies through combining two signal transduction channels into one method has been proposed and drawn considerable attention. The integration of two sensing signals not only promotes the analytical efficiency with reduced assumption, but also improves the analytical performances with enlarged detection linear range, enhanced accuracy, and boosted application flexibility. The two top-rated output signals for developing dual-modal sensors are colorimetric and fluorescent signals because of their outstanding merits for point of care applications and real-time sensitive sensing. Given the rapid development of material chemistry and nanotechnology, the recent decade has witnessed great advance in colorimetric/fluorimetric signal based dual-modal sensing technologies. The new sensing strategy leads to a broad avenue for various applications in disease diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety because of the complementary and synergistic effects of the two output signals. In this state-of-the-art review, we comprehensively summarize different types of colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensing methods by highlighting representative research in the last 5 years, digging into their sensing methodologies, particularly the working principles of the signal transduction systems. Then, the challenges and future prospects for boosting further development of this research field are discussed.

The advantages of colorimetry/fluorimetry-based dual-modal sensing technologies.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Exploration of the existence, distribution or concentration of certain analytes enabled by sensing technologies plays a fundamental role in biochemical analysis including medical diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety (Kolanowski et al., 2018). Longstanding and unremitting efforts have been devoted to develop new sensing methods for covering various ever-increasing demands (work well in sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, robustness, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendliness) from related scientific research or practical applications (Brothers et al., 2019; Heikenfeld et al., 2019). For example, ultrasensitive sensing technologies have been developed for ultra-trace detection and early diagnosis (Huang et al., 2016); multiplex sensing technologies have been designed for simultaneous detection of multiple targets in one sample (Wu et al., 2020a).

However, the conventional sensing methods are based on single output signal channel and inevitably limited by various instinct shortcomings of the signal transduction systems (Chen et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2020). For example, colorimetric sensing methods exhibit great potential in point-of-care testing (POCT) due to their merits of cost-effectiveness, simplicity and naked-eye signal readout, while always confront the shortcoming of low detection sensitivity (Qi et al., 2020). The fluorimetric methods can achieve high sensitivity for in vivo or in vitro real-time sensing applications, but are limited by some drawbacks such as low tissue penetration ability of ultraviolet excitation, high background from biomolecules and insufficient spatial resolution (Cheng et al., 2020; Di Wu et al., 2020). Using fluorophores with large-wavelength emissions (e.g. NIR dyes), large Stokes shifts (e.g. quantum dots [QDs]), anti-Stokes shifts (e.g. up-conversion nanoparticles [UCNPs]), or long lifetimes (e.g. lanthanide-based materials) can relieve such defects but will cause certain problems, such as reduced signal intensity and increased dependency toward high-end instruments (Meyer et al., 2014; Savchuk et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2020). Therefore, single-modal sensing methods are lacking in adaptive capacity for practical applications; thus, two or more methods may be needed to address different demands. For example, the rapid colorimetric method tested positive samples are quantified by the high-sensitive fluorimetric methods (Brangel et al., 2018; Fujioka et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2017). This multi-method sensing strategy can provide results with enhanced confidence level, however, also increases the consumption of time, labor, samples, reagents and instruments.

In recent years, with the development of material chemistry and nanotechnology, several sensing methods integrated with two signal transduction channels have been developed (Wen et al., 2016). Dual-modal sensing technologies can compensate the intrinsic limitations of each sensing modality, and integrate their unique advantages, thereby exhibiting some synergistic effects on the analytical performances (Hong et al., 2018; Wang et al. 2019, 2020b). First, the combination of two signal output channels in parallel in one method can provide more convincing results than corresponding single-modal methods at a low consumption of time, labor, and reagents (Oh et al., 2020). The dual-modal sensing method can not only achieve enhanced reliability by mutual-verification of two signals, but also obtain improved robustness due to the decreased negative impact of some interference that can reduce the accuracy of the two output signals (Kim and Hong, 2019). Second, dual-modal sensing technologies exhibit reinforced flexibility in practical applications due to the diversity of the two optional signals. The differences in sensitivity, detection linear range and signal decoding of the two integrated signal outputs enable dual-modal sensing technologies to address different sensing issues flexibly (Chen et al., 2019). For example, a dual-modal sensing method with one signal, which is naked-eye colorimetric signal, and a highly sensitive instrument-decoding signal exhibits high acceptance for both on-site screening in resource-constrained regions and accurate analysis in a lab. In addition, the enlarged detection linear range enhances the adaptability for more analysis targets (Koyappayil et al., 2021). Given such charming merits, the past decades have witnessed high-speed evolution of dual-modal sensing technologies (He et al., 2020). Among various output signals, colorimetric and fluorescent signals are the two most widely used signals and have been considered as the common transduction channels in developing dual-modal sensing technologies owing to their advantages. The colorimetric signal is favored because of its simple, low-cost, and naked-eye readout, whereas fluorescent signal enables real-time, highly sensitive, and multiplexing quantification due to its short response time, high signal intensity, and colorful display (Liu et al., 2020). Given the above-mentioned advantages of joint use in parallel with another output signal, numerous colorimetry/fluorimetry based dual-modal sensing technologies have already been developed for food safety (Cui et al., 2020), environmental monitoring (Jiang et al., 2020) and clinical diagnosis (Sun et al., 2018).

Hence, in this review, we try to comprehensively cover recent published works on colorimetry/fluorimetry-based dual-modal sensing technologies from methodology to applications (Scheme 1 ). Initially, we summarized the general principles of developing colorimetry/fluorimetry-based dual-modal sensing technologies (section 2). Subsequently, the dual-modal sensing technologies incorporating colorimetric and fluorescent signals are all-sided reviewed at section 3. Then dual-modal sensing technologies involving colorimetric signal and another output signals (except for signal) are introduced in section 4. Dual-modal sensing technologies involving fluorimetric signal and other output signals (apart from colorimetric signal) are summarized in section 5. Some examples for intracellular or in vivo applications are also highlighted in this part. Finally, the challenges and future perspective of designing next generation colorimetry/fluorimetry-based dual-modal sensing technologies are presented in section 6. With the comprehensive understanding on the design mechanisms of colorimetry/fluorimetry-based dual-modal sensing technologies, this review can not only provide a brief and valid guideline for selecting suitable sensing technologies according to particular applications, but also facilitate the development of this hot research area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General principle for developing dual-modal sensing technologies
ce:section.ce:para: The core of dual-modal sensing, that is, the main improvement over single-modal sensing, is the signal reporting system capable of outputting two different types of signals. The dual-signal output system is commonly designed on the basis of the dual-functional probes. These dual-functional probes can generate two different output signals, and both signal intensities can be regulated by the concentration of target analytes. Based on the difference of design principle, the dual-functional probes can be classified into two major types, including single-unit detection probe and dual-unit detection probe.

The single-unit detection probe refers to a probe containing a single component capable of generating or regulating two different signals simultaneously (Yuan et al., 2007). These components include organic dyes (Avirah et al., 2007), polymers (Nawaz et al., 2018), and nanomaterials (Zhou and Ma, 2017). For example, curcumin is a phenolic compound naturally found in the ginger family, and its spectra of light absorption and fluorescence emission exhibit a strong allochroic effect under different pH conditions (Tsvetkov et al., 2005). Therefore, curcumin shows great potential application for constructing single-unit detection probe. Miao et al. reported a colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensor for cardiac troponin I (cTnI) detection by loading numerous curcumin molecules to MoS2 nanomaterial as the detection probe (Miao et al., 2018). In the sensing system, curcumin is used as a dual-signal reporter to generate colorimetric and fluorescent signals, whereas MoS2 is used as the curcumin container to amplify the detection signal. Similarly, some other organic dyes, such as fluorescein with fluorescence and peroxidase-mimicking properties (Liu et al., 2016), oxidized tetramethyl benzidine (ox-TMB) with colorimetry and photothermal conversion properties (Yi et al., 2020), and oxidized 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) with colorimetric and photoacoustic imaging properties (Ding et al., 2018b), can be used in developing a single-unit detection probe. However, these organic dyes are usually required to integrate with a signal amplification carrier to improve the detection sensitivity of dual-modal sensors because of their low signal intensity. In addition, some polymers modified with the fluorescence chromogenic groups are used to design the single-unit detection probe based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effects (Tian and Xu, 2007). Compared with organic dyes and functional polymers, nanomaterials have received considerable attention for designing single-unit detection probes because of their diverse signal output channel, high signal intensity, and easy conjugation with bio-distinguished elements (e.g., antibodies and DNA sequences) (Sapsford et al., 2011; Sivaram et al., 2018). For instance, Lin's group adopted MnO2 nanoflowers as a single-unit detection probe of an immunochromatographic test strip (ICTS) for pesticide residue detection (Ouyang et al., 2018a). In this signal conduction system, the characteristic color of MnO2 nanoflowers is used as the colorimetric signal, and its biomimetic catalysis activity is used to output chemiluminescence (CL) signal by catalyzing the luminol-H2O2 system. Analogously, iron oxide NPS (Xiao et al., 2018), noble metal NP (Zhou and Ma, 2017), hybrid metal NP (Wang et al., 2018d), and carbon-based nanomaterials (Shen et al., 2017) capable of generating two signals are used to establish single-unit detection probes. Table 1 provides the design fundamentals, applications, and advantage/disadvantages of some representative single-unit detection probes.

The dual-unit detection probe refers to a probe containing two different components for the generation or regulation of two detection signals. Compared with a single-unit detection probe, a dual-unit detection probe flexibly integrated with two signal output units can reveal more signal response pathways to address diverse analytical demands. The dual-unit probes can be classified into target-responsive and target-independent dual-unit probe, depending on whether their structure and initial properties for signal generation/regulation were changed directly or indirectly by the targets or not. Target-independent probes always serve as labels of an assay (e.g., immunoassays), where a stoichiometric relation exists between the probe concentration and target concentration based on binding events (He et al., 2018). For example, Bai et al. designed a dual-unit detection probe via integrating surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signals onto colorimetric nanomaterials for cTnI detection on the ICTS platform (Bai et al., 2018). The dual-unit detection probe was synthesized by modifying numerous Nile blue A molecules on the surface of silver-coated gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) nanocomposite (AuNP@Ag); then, the AuNP@Ag nanocomposite was further coated with an Au layer to enhance the SERS signal of Nile blue A. When meeting with target or target-derived active molecules (e.g., H2O2 in glucose oxidase [GOx] catalyzed glucose sensing system, •OH produced by horseradish peroxidase [HRP] based immunoassays), target-responsive probes exert an allosteric effect on one unit or unit assembly/disassembly. FRET is a common principle in fabricating a responsive dual-unit probe, which usually exhibits a “domino effect” induced by a target-triggered absorbance change of the acceptor (Yan et al., 2018). Zhang et al. used anti-carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) antibody-labeled magnetic beads as capture probes to quench the fluorescence signals on the nitrocellulose (NC) membrane for CEA detection (Zhang et al., 2017). In their experiment, the Cy5-labeled anti-CEA detection antibodies were sprayed on the NC membrane as the test (T) line, and when the CEA mediated the sandwich structure of the magnetic probe and the antibodies were immobilized on the T line, the fluorescence signals from the Cy5 molecules were quenched by the colorimetric signals from magnetic beads. In this sensor, the colorimetric and fluorescent signal units are not integrated onto one nanoplatform, but they are assembled on the NC membrane via target analyte-mediated combination. In recent years, dual-unit detection probe has received considerable attention in developing colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensors because of its diverse signal generation pathways (Gao et al., 2017). Therefore, we have listed some characteristic examples in Table 2 to provide a comparative discussion, including their sensing units, signal generation pathways, and advantages/disadvantages.

The integration of colorimetric and fluorimetric signals in one method exhibits enhanced sensing accuracy and high flexibility in switch between POCT and highly sensitive detection. Meanwhile, the close correlation between light absorbance and fluorescent emission has boosted the development of colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal signal transduction reporters/systems (Tian et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020a; Würth et al., 2013). Therefore, among all the dual-modal sensing technologies, colorimetric-fluorimetric type is the most popular. Based on the different strategies of target-induced dual-signal generation, the colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensing technologies can be categorized into three classifications, including target analyte direct stimulation, enzyme-mediated activation, and bio-recognition component-mediated strategy.

In this sensing system, the target analytes directly interact with the detection probe to cause an allosteric effect or aggregate/decompose reactions of the probe, thereby stimulating the colorimetric and fluorescent signal variation. This strategy is simple and straightforward, and this kind of colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensor has short detection time, low cost, and simple operation procedure (Duan et al., 2018). Therefore, it has gained great attention in large-scale routine screening analysis, POCT, and other rapid detection fields (Deng et al., 2015; Mohammadpour et al., 2017). In the past decade, a large number of target analyte directly activating dual-modal sensors have been proposed for various target analyte detection, such as ions (Yan et al., 2018), gas molecules (Deng et al., 2015), reactive oxygen species (Mohammadpour et al., 2017), small organic molecules (Ren et al., 2018), polymers (Lu et al., 2017), and pH (Chen et al., 2018a). For example, Zhang et al. utilized dinitrophenyl ether-modified tetraphenylethylene as a detection probe for dual-modal sensing of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) (Zhang et al., 2015). The H2S molecule in the solution could react selectively with the dinitrophenyl group to induce the allosteric effect of the detection probe, thereby resulting in the colorimetric development and strong aggregation-induced emission (AIE) from the produced 2,4-dinitrobenzenethiol and tetraphenylethylene-OH. The chelating reaction has been widely used to design this type of sensor to achieve the detection of metal ions. In this regard, Nawaz et al. chemically bound a Fe2+-specific fluorimetric reporter of 1, 10-phenanthroline-5-amine (Phen) onto cellulose acetate (CA) using 4, 4′-methylene diphenyl diisocyanate (MDI) as a cross-linker to propose a polymer-based colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensor (Phen-MDI-CA) for Fe2+ detection (Fig. 1 A) (Nawaz et al., 2018). Phen-MDI-CA showed strong blue fluorescence because of the high loading intensity of Phen. In the presence of Fe2+, an insoluble Fe-(Phen-MDI-CA) complex was formed, which exhibited quenched fluorescence because of the nonradiative electron or energy transfer from Phen to Fe2+. Meanwhile, the Fe-(Phen-MDI-CA) complex displayed a red color and an absorption peak at 529 nm because of the strong “metal-to-ligand charge transfer” absorption. Compared with the molecular-based detection probe, nanomaterials (Gao et al., 2018), such as QDs (Wang et al., 2018b), silver nanoparticle (AgNP) (Huang et al., 2018), and AuNP (Zheng et al., 2013), are becoming popular because of their excellent physicochemical properties. In general, noble metal NPs (e.g., AuNPs and AgNPs) exhibit extinction coefficients 1000-fold higher than traditional organic dyes, provided by their unique localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) property (Vasimalai and John, 2013). Moreover, noble metal nanomaterials can serve as an efficient fluorescence quencher or enhancer by utilizing FRET or metal-enhanced fluorescence effect, thereby promoting the development of colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensors (Mohammadpour et al., 2017). For instance, Zheng et al. presented a colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal lysozyme nanosensor, where fluorimetric triazolylcoumarin was readily bound onto AuNPs via electrostatic interaction (Zheng et al., 2013). The target lysozyme has a strong positive charge, and it can exert electrostatic forces on the surfaces of AuNPs. Therefore, in the presence of lysozyme, some triazolylcoumarin molecules will be removed from the surfaces of AuNPs to obtain recovered fluorescence; meanwhile, the deprotected AuNPs will change the color of the solution from red to purple by triazolylcoumarin-induced aggregation (Fig. 1B).

Certain types of analytes, including enzymes, enzyme substrates (e.g., glucose), and enzyme inhibitors (e.g., pesticide residue), can indirectly activate the colorimetric-fluorimetric signal variations of probes through corresponding enzymatic reactions. Given the selectivity and enzyme-mediated signal amplification, this dual-modal sensor always shows high specificity and gratifying detection sensitivity (Xiong et al., 2018b). At present, various natural enzymes including acetylcholinesterase (Liu et al., 2012), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) (Zhang et al., 2016), tyrosinase (Zhao et al., 2017), telomerase (Fu et al., 2018a), and glucose oxidase (GOx) (Liu et al., 2018a), have been used to develop dual-modal sensing technologies. For example, Fu et al. designed a portable colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensor for highly sensitive detection of telomerase in the cells with a LOD of 20 cells/μL (Fu et al., 2018a). The substrate primer was immobilized onto a cellulose paper, and UCNP and methylene blue labeled with telomeric complementary oligonucleotide were used as the fluorimetric and colorimetric reporters. In the presence of telomerase, the substrate primer was extended, and the two signal reporters were hybridized by the elongated substrate sequence, thereby resulting in the dramatically increased colorimetric/fluorimetric signals. Enzyme substrates can also be used as a colorimetric/fluorimetric signal regulator, which were detected using this strategy. For example, Rao's group utilized GOx labeled with the core-satellite assemblies as the colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal reporter for glucose detection (Liu et al., 2018a). The core-satellite nanocomposite was constructed by conjugating carbon nanodots (C-dots) onto the core Au@Ag NPs. The glucose in the serum was oxidized into H2O2 by GOx on the reporter, and the Ag shell of Au@Ag NPs was etched into Ag+, leading to the peeling of the satellite C-dots away from Au@Ag NPs. The LSPR change of Au@Ag NPs enabled colorimetric detection of glucose, whereas the recovered fluorescence of C-dots served as a second output signal (Fig. 1C). Except for natural enzymes, nanozymes with high stability and low cost have also received considerable interests in developing colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensors (Wang et al., 2017; Xiao et al., 2018). However, the low selectivity of nanozymes to the substrate hinders the development of colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensing technologies.

Target analytes that hardly participate in biochemical reactions are usually monitored by quantifying specific bio-recognition interactions mediated by binding events (Hu et al., 2017). For example, the traditional immunoassay is a commonly used platform for designing the colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensing system because of its high specificity, easy operation, and high-throughput detection (Hu et al., 2017). In our previous study, we developed a colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal immunoassay for the ultrasensitive detection of alpha fetal protein (AFP) in human serum (Zhou et al., 2018). In our work, a sulfhydryl-amphiphilic ligand with modification of gold nanoflower was synthesized to encapsulate fluorescein. Under pH regulation, this nanoprobe can release fluorescein on alkaline conditions, and then, the released fluorescein can be used for fluorescence signal output, whereas the intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity of fluorescein can be used for colorimetric detection via catalyzing TMB into ox-TMB (Fig. 1D). Similarly, other bio-recognition systems, such as biotin–streptavidin (Nargang et al., 2016), aptamer-receptor (Duan et al., 2018), and DNA hybridization (Yu et al., 2017), can be used as a colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensor. For example, Lu et al. used a classical nanoflare model to design an aptamer-based colorimetric-fluorimetric dual-modal sensor for the detection of adenosine triphosphate (Lu et al., 2018). The nanoflare was fabricated by the aptamer-modified FAM dye and aptamer complementary sequence-coated AuNP. If the samples contained adenosine triphosphate, then the FAM-labeled aptamer can capture adenosine triphosphate and then separate from the surface of AuNP to release fluorescence signals. In addition, with the separation of aptamer and AuNP, high concentration of NaCl solution can induce the aggregation of AuNP to generate colorimetric signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-modal sensing technologies involving colorimetric signal and another output signal
ce:section.ce:para: Except for a fluorescence signal, other types of output signals have also been integrated to develop various dual-modal sensing technologies, which have been widely used in food safety (Wu et al., 2018), environmental monitoring (Khoshroo et al., 2018), clinical diagnosis (Cheng et al., 2019) and so forth. In this section, we focus on the design strategies of the dual-modal sensing technologies involving colorimetric signal and another output signals, such as CL, electrochemical (EC) signal, SERS, pressure, or temperature, particularly the working principle of dual-functional probes.

CL is a widely used photon emission excited by a chemical reaction rather than an external light source, which can attain high signal-to-background ratio because of the absence of light scattering (Song et al., 2019). Therefore, the combination of CL can compensate the insufficient sensitivity of colorimetric sensing technologies (Bagheri et al., 2019). At present, the H2O2-luminol system is the widely used CL system, which can be catalyzed by HRP to produce oxidized luminol, a strong CL product (Zhang et al., 2018b). Hence, a simple way to generate CL is introducing HRP as a signal regulator. For example, Jiang's group reported a colorimetric-CL ICTS for the simultaneous detection of CEA and procalcitonin, wherein HRP and antibody co-labeled AuNPs were used as dual-functional probes (Chen et al., 2016). The accumulation of the probes at the test line mediated by target antigens leads to a vivid red color derived from LSPR of AuNPs and CL signals generated by the labeled HRP-catalyzed CL reaction of luminol. In addition, peroxidase-mimicking nanozyme is a potential alternative to HRP in catalyzing the H2O2-luminol system because of its high catalytic activity (Ouyang et al., 2018a). For example, Ouyang et al. synthesized a graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth ferrite nanocomposite (g-C3N4/BiFeO3 NC) to develop a colorimetric-CL dual-modal sensor (Ouyang et al., 2018b). The ICTS with competitive formation was established by using anti-pesticide antibody-labeled g-C3N4/BiFeO3 NC as the dual-signal probes. When the sample solution was added, the probes were migrated into the test zone and captured by the antigen on the membrane to obtain brown g-C3N4/BiFeO3 NC for colorimetric detection, and the intrinsic peroxidase-mimicking activity of g-C3N4/BiFeO3 NC on the T line was used to catalyze H2O2 and luminol for CL detection (Fig. 2 A). However, the application of nanozymes in immunoassays needs modification of target-specific receptors, including antibody, which will decrease the catalytic activity of nanozymes because of the unavoidable surface passivation (Fu et al., 2020). Researchers also tried to use luminol-coated AuNPs as dual-modal reporters; however, the CL signal intensity was limited by the small loading capacity of AuNPs for luminol molecules (Ouyang et al. 2018c, 2018d). Compared with the traditional CL signal, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) generated by electro-catalyzed redox reaction can propose higher detection sensitivity (Liu et al., 2015b). The colored nanomaterials with electrocatalytic activity, such as noble metal NPs and graphene oxide, were commonly used to design colorimetric-ECL reporters (Li et al., 2017b; Zhang et al., 2013). In addition, the high-energy electrons generated by electro-catalytic reaction can be used to oxidize some common colorimetric substrates (e.g., ABTS and TMB), thereby facilitating colorimetric-ECL dual-modal sensors. For example, Xu et al. developed a colorimetric-ECL Hg2+ sensor by using Au@Pt NPs as electrocatalysts (Xu et al., 2018). The high electrocatalytic activity of Au@Pt NPs promoted the conversion of H2O2 to •OH, thereby facilitating the oxidation of TMB and luminol for colorimetric and ECL signals.

EC signal is another attractive second output signal for colorimetric methods due to its high sensitivity, and excellent applicability for muddy or colored samples. Generally, colorimetric and EC dual-modal sensing technologies can be easily achieved by considering the redox reactions that can generate a colored product, such as HRP catalyzed oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 (Tanaka et al., 2018). The target antigen induced accumulation of HRP-antibody conjugates onto the electrode can catalyze the generation of ox-TMB and lead to a variation in current. The two types of signals can also derived from two separate signal transduction systems. Zhang and co-workers developed a dual-channel homogeneous senor for the detection of thrombin in blood samples using a DNAzyme-based colorimetric transduction system and a hydroxyapatite particle (HAP)-based EC transduction system (Fig. 2B). In particular, in the presence of target thrombin, HAP will be captured by magnetic NPs through thrombin aptamers on the surfaces of the two particles, whereas the colorimetric ssDNA probe pre-combined with aptamers on magnetic NPs will be removed (Zhang et al., 2018a). After magnetic separation, the reaction between HAP NPs and Na2MoO4 will deposit redox-active molybdophosphate precipitates onto electrode and provide a sensitive EC signal for thrombin detection with a LOD of 0.4 fM. The colorimetric ssDNA probe in the supernatant can bind with hemin to form quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, which can catalyze TMB oxidation to generate a colorimetric signal. Photo-EC (PEC) sensing has been rapidly developed by virtue of its excellent analytical performance, low-cost equipment, and easy miniaturization (Zhao et al., 2014). Considering that, the quantification signal of photocurrent will not be disturbed by noise optical signal induced by excitation, high detection sensitivity can be easily achieved. Wei et al. developed an immunoassay for the detection of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) using ALP as a label and regulator for colorimetric and PEC signals (Wei et al., 2018). Ascorbic acid (AA) generated from ALP-catalyzed reduction of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) is not only used as an electron donor to capture the holes in CdS/ZnO hollow nanorod arrays, leading to the increase in photocurrent, but also used to reduce Ag+ into silver shells on Au nanobipyramids, leading to a blue shift in the LSPR peak (Fig. 2C). Despite the high sensitivity and wide linear detection range, an enzymatic reaction based sensor suffers from high cost, limited robustness, and tedious enzyme immobilization. Hence, some efforts have been given into designing and exploiting non-enzymatic sensors based on electrocatalysis (Chen et al., 2018b). Wang and co-workers developed a non-enzymatic colorimetric-EC sensor for the detection of MCF-7 cells (Wang et al., 2015), where three-dimensional reduced graphene oxide and AuNPs were consecutively modified onto the electrode, and polyhedral AuPd alloy NPs with excellent catalytic performance were employed as tracing labels. The H2O2 generated in MCF-7 cells would be in-situ catalyzed converted into •OH by the attached polyhedral AuPd alloy NPs, thereby producing an amplified EC signal. The resultant •OH was then utilized to oxidize TMB to produce chromogenic product.

Compared with conventional colorimetric output based on traditional organic dyes, a plasmonic signal based on noble metal NPs such as AuNPs or AgNPs, shows dramatically enhanced sensitivity due to their high light extinction coefficients (Lin et al., 2011). Moreover, the versatile LSPR engineering of noble metal NPs (size, shape, composition, interparticle distance, and surrounding dielectric environment) facilitates various highly sensitive naked-eye colorimetric sensing methods (Klajn et al., 2009). Meanwhile, the LSPR of noble metal nanomaterials enables massive enhancement of fingerprint-like Raman signals of the nearby reporter molecules, which is endowed as the SERS effect (Herzog et al., 2013; Im et al., 2013; Visaveliya and Köhler, 2015). Therefore, various colorimetric-SERS dual-modal sensors based on noble metal NPs have been proposed for flexible applications in field detection and trace analysis. For example, Feng et al. developed an AuNPs probe for colorimetric-SERS dual-modal detection of telomerase activity based on the controllable switch between assembly and aggregation statement of the probes (Feng et al., 2018). First, an Au dimer-based probe with high uniformity was assembled through the hybridization of two DNA segments, displaying enhanced SERS signal and strong LSPR at 610 nm. In the presence of telomerase, the DNA primer extension with repeat units would complementarily hybridized with one of the two AuNPs in the dimer, thus leading to the disaggregation of Au dimers. Hence, the telomerase activity was reflected by the decrease of Raman signal and blue shift of LSPR signal that can be naked-eye readout. Guo et al. proposed a glass chip immunoassay using gold nanowire (AuNW) assembled gold vesicles as dual-modal reporters for highly sensitive detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Fig. 3 A). The SERS and LSPR signals were dramatically promoted by silver stain process, leading to enhanced detection sensitivity (Guo et al., 2018). Besides of the above-mentioned strategy of “hot spot” engineering, colorimetric-SERS sensing can also be developed utilizing the conversion or attachment of reporters at the “hot spots”. Li et al. designed a headspace-sampling paper-based device for colorimetric-SERS dual-modal sensing of SO2 in wine (Li et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 3B, the smart device was fabricated with a vacuum filtration method, where 4-mercaptopyridine (Mpy)-modified gold nanorod-graphene oxide (AuNRs-rGO) hybrids (rGO/MPy-AuNRs), deep blue colored starch-iodine complex (SIC) were immobilized into cellulose-based filter papers. Target SO2 extracted from the headspace sample solution would trigger the Karl Fischer reaction, resulting in color fading with the dissociation of SIC, and increase of SERS signals derived from the SO2-triggered conversion of Mpy to pyridine methyl sulfate on the AuNRs.

In recent years, sensors based on signals of pressure and temperature that can be readily and accurately read out according to the liquid level scale have received increasing attention for POC detection (Ge et al., 2019; Tao et al., 2017). Compared with colorimetric signal, pressure and temperature signals are more suitable for muddy or colored samples. Therefore, the combination of colorimetric and pressure/temperature signals provides enhanced feasibility in POC applications, as well as enhanced sensitivity and accuracy sometimes.

Noble metal NPs also exhibit high photothermal conversion efficiency, thereby displaying high thermal/temperature signal output capacity (Deng et al., 2017). Liu's group presented an ALP-based ELISA system, where the colorimetric signal and temperature outputted by a probe of gold nanostars are regulated by ALP-catalyzed silver deposition (Fig. 3C). The antigen-mediated introduction of ALP leads to the conversion of AAP to AA and then induces the deposition of silver onto gold nanostars. The silver deposition would causes a large blue shift in the LSPR of gold nanostars, and meanwhile decrease the photothermal conversion efficiency under 808 nm NIR laser due to the reduced absorbance of probes (Liu et al., 2018b). The proposed ELISA provided colorimetric sensing of prostatic antigen (PSA) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.53 ng/mL, and an evident temperature decrease of 1 °C was observed even at a target concentration of 1.0 ng/mL. Fu et al. developed an immunoassay by using iron oxide nanozymes as labels to catalyze the oxidation of TMB (Fu et al., 2018b). The strong absorption at 650 nm of the generated ox-TMB enabled highly sensitive naked-eye detection of PSA; the ox-TMB also can serve as efficient photothermal conversion agents for visual quantitative readout (LOD of 1.0 ng/mL for PSA) with a common thermometer. The generation of gas in some reactions, for instance, O2 generated from the composition of H2O2, can be measured by the change in pressure. Ding et al. established a colorimetric-pressure sensor for H2O2 detection based on the peroxidase- and catalase-like catalytic activity of MnCo2O4 nanotubes (Fig. 3D). Being labeled with folic acid, the nanozymes could be captured by cancer cells (e.g. NIH3T3 cells, A549 cells, and HeLa cells) which are H2O2-abundant, then oxidized TMB was produced displaying with blue color, and O2 generated from nanozyme catalyzed H2O2 decomposition leads to a change in pressure (Ding et al., 2018a).

Fluorimetric sensing technology is a popular spectroscopic method playing a major role in in-vitro sensing and real-time in vivo/cell imaging (Jeong et al., 2018; Nsibande and Forbes, 2016). The integration of a second signal has been regarded as a feasible solution to compensate some disadvantages of fluorimetry sensing (e.g., low spatial resolution) or provide enhanced detection sensitivity, accuracy, and reliability. In this part, we are reviewing the recent advances of fluorimetric-based dual-modal sensing technologies, focusing on the designing/working principle of the dual-modal signal output probes/systems, and synergistic effect between fluorescent signal and the second signal output. Specifically, fluorimetric-based dual-modal sensing technologies with various second signal transduction channels including SERS, EC, magnetism, chirality, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), pressure, and size, will be discussed in detail.

The integration of fluorimetric and SERS signals is generally based on the modulation effect (e.g., SERS, and MEF) of noble metal NPs for fluorimetric and Raman signals (Kohr et al., 2017; Yaraki et al., 2020). For example, Wang et al. developed a metal enhanced fluorimetric-SERS dual-modal reporter by conjugating CdTe QDs onto SERS NPs of Au@Ag-Raman dye@silica (Wang et al., 2012). Typically, a synergistic effect on the multiplexing ability was achieved with a huge encoding capacity. Some organic dyes can act as “two-in-one” reporters to simultaneously output fluorimetric and Raman signals. As an example, Gabudean et al. used cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide stabilized AuNRs as substrate material to enhance the fluorimetric and Raman signal intensities of Rose Bengal, which would be adsorbed onto AuNR via electrostatic interaction (Gabudean et al., 2012). This probe could be extensively used to detect targets with stronger bonding affinity toward AuNRs than cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide. Yang and co-workers developed a fluorimetric-SERS dual-modal imaging-guided tumor-cell-specific drug delivery system (Yang et al., 2014). In this design, doxorubicin (DOX) loaded mesoporous silica NPs were functionalized with folic acid to exert tumor specific release of DOX. Meanwhile, the released DOX acted as the fluorescence-Raman dual-signal reporter, whose signal was enhanced by using pre-internalized silver NP as the hotspot provider in cancer cells (Fig. 4 A). The distance between plasmonic substrate and fluorimetric/Raman dyes is crucial for the signal output. Zou et al. proposed an immunoassay for the detection of a tuberculosis antigen (CFP-10), where magnetoplasmonic nanowires were synthesized as carriers, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as fluorimetric-Raman reporters (Fig. 4B). In the presence of CFP-10, GQDs would be brought close to magnetoplasmonic nanowires, thereby outputting enhanced fluorescence and Raman signals (Zou et al., 2015).

On the contrary, SERS and fluorimetry agents were spatially isolated in the fluorimetric-SERS Janus microgels proposed by Xu's group (Yue et al., 2017). 4-mercaptobenzoic acid-modified AuNPs and C-dots were separately encapsulated into the two hemispheres of the Janus microgels. The proposed Janus microgels were used for dual-modal pH sensing by taking advantage of the particles distance change due to pH variation induced swelling-shrinking behavior of microgels, pH sensitive fluorescence of C-dots and Raman spectra change derived from pH-related COOH/COO− conversion in 4-mercaptobenzoic acid.

EC signal enables highly sensitive detection, and shows higher suitability for turbid samples compared with fluorescent signal, thereby has been considered as an attractive option for developing fluorimetric-based dual-modal sensors (Chang et al., 2020). Typically, fluorimetric-EC dual-modal sensing technologies can be developed on the basis of EC active and fluorescence substances (Koo et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015). Mars et al. coated curcumin, a widely used fluorimetric reporter that has also demonstrated with EC behavior (Mars et al., 2018), onto GQDs-ITO transparent electrode to develop a fluorimetric-EC sensor (Fig. 4C). By covalently attaching DNA probe to the curcumin on the electrode, they developed a dual-modal sensor for the detection of Alzheimer's disease responsible biomarker of APO e4 DNA. Upon in situ hybridization of the target DNA on the electrode, the fluorescence of the sensor decreased due to the DNA complex-induced quenching of the photoelectron transfer process, thus providing sensitive fluorimetric sensing with a LOD of 12.4 pg/mL. Meanwhile, the complex also acted an inert kinetic barrier for the electron-transfer of curcumin to generate an amperometric signal for target DNA sensing with a LOD of 2.18 pg/mL. Similarly, Maiga et al. established a glucose-sensing platform based on brilliant-green (BG) sequestered and GOx-immobilized poly (amic) acid membranes (Maiga et al., 2018). In the presence of glucose, the consumption of dissolved oxygen in GOx-catalyzed glucose oxidation reaction would eliminate oxygen-mediated fluorescence quench of BG, thereby resulting in increased fluorescent intensity. Meanwhile, the membrane was fixed steadily with glassy carbon electrode by an O-ring for the measurement of an amperometric signal attributed to the GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose.

A homogeneous EC sensor based on different pulse voltammetry signals has attracted tremendous interest, because of its advantages including free of probe immobilization, simplicity, rapidity and low price. Karakus et al. devised an electroactive AIE dye for selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ ion in aqueous media, through simple combination of redox active ferrocenecarboxaldehyde and 2-aminoanthracene (Karakuş et al., 2020). The resultant probe of ANT-Fe displayed strong green AIE fluorescence owing to the poor water solubility, irreversible electrode reaction and rapid Cu2+-active hydrolysis at the CN moiety. Upon the interaction with guest Cu2+, the probe broke into 2-aminoanthracene and ferrocenecarboxaldehyde, leading to fluorescence “turn-off” and reversible electrode reaction with a cathodic potential shift of 50 mV. Chang et al. proposed a homogeneous sensor for adenosine triphosphate (ATP) detection based on target triggered cargo release system, zeolitic imidazolate framework-90 (ZIF-90) loaded with an electroactive AIE dye of methylene blue. The formed probes exerted ATP-specific disassembly behavior because of the competitive coordination between ATP and imidazolate-2-carboxyaldehyde on Zn2+ in the ZIF structure. Then, the released methylene blue showed strong fluorescence at 683 nm under the 610 nm excitation and displayed a significant oxidation peak at −0.23 V, thus enabling label-free, enzyme-free, ultrasensitive, and dual-signal sensing of ATP (Chang et al., 2020).

Fluorimetric imaging is favored as a highly sensitive real-time visualization technique, but is limited by its poor spatial resolution and low tissue penetrability (Li et al., 2017a). On the contrary, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) shows high spatial resolution and tissue penetrability but insufficient sensitivity (Chen et al., 2014). Hence, the combination of fluorescent imaging and MRI has gained considerable attention, and various fluorimetric-magnetic nanomaterials have been proposed for dual-modal imaging or imaging guided therapy/surgery. He et al. co-encapsulated CdSe@CdS QDs and Fe3O4 NPs in a microemulsion system to form hybrid nanoprobes (He et al., 2018). The nanoprobe exhibited excellent colloidal stability, bright fluorescent emission and high saturation magnetization, thus making it competent for in vitro fluorescence and MRI of cancer cells. Compared with traditional organic dyes and QDs, UCNPs with unique anti-Stokes shifts, when used as imaging agents, possess the advantages of deeper tissue penetration capacity due to the application of NIR excitation and enhanced signal-to-noise ratio because of the elimination of endogenous fluorescence interference (Zhu et al., 2017). Lv et al. synthesized MnO2 nanosheets coated NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaYF4:Yb, Nd dual-functional nanoprobes (UCNP@MnO2) for the sensing of intracellular glutathione (GSH)/H2O2 level (Lv et al., 2018), which could be used to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. This hierarchical multilevel UCNP–MnO2 nanoprobe can lead to the decomposition of MnO2 sheets into paramagnetic Mn2+ ions when exposed to a microenvironment containing high level of GSH/H2O2, which can be monitored by the restored fluorescence of UCNP the MRI signal of Mn2+ (Fig. 4D).

Except for in vivo/intracellular imaging, fluorimetric-magnetic nanomaterials have also been used for in vitro detection based on dual-signal modalities of fluorescence and magnetic relaxation. Moreover, magnetic separation can minimize the matrix interference of complex samples and specifically concentrating target analytes, thus proposing one or two orders of magnitude enhanced detection sensitivity (He et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2021). Banerjee et al. established an immunoassay for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7, typical pathogenic bacteria, using a core-shell magneto-fluorimetric nanoprobe, which was prepared by encapsulating a lipophilic dye of DiI within the polyacrylic acid coatings of antibody-conjugated iron NPs (Banerjee et al., 2016). The bond between target bacteria and nanoprobes can directly reflect the variation of magnetic relaxation, which also can be measured by the fluorescence signal after removing the unbounded nanoprobes via centrifugation. The magnetic relaxation property of the nanoprobe enables ultrasensitive screening at low target concentration with a LOD of 1 CFU/mL, which is complemented by fluorescence measurements of samples at high concentrations.

Chiral metamaterials can lead to several orders of magnitude higher circular dichroism (CD) intensity than that of chiral biomolecules, and have gained substantial research interest in developing nanosensors with ultrahigh sensitivity (10−15-10−21 M) (Kneer et al., 2018). In particular, the integration of optical chirality and fluorescence in a nanosystem has been regarded as a charming strategy for optically active components in biosensors. Chiral fluorimetric nanomaterials may display strong coupling with incident photons, and can potentially bring unusual optical responses for chiral and fluorimetric sensing (Nakashima et al., 2009). Ahmed et al. produced ultrasmall chiral blue-emitting molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) QDs from MoS2 nanopowder using AA as a reducing agent (Ahmed and Neethirajan 2018). A proposed immunoassay based on the reporter of MoS2 QDs and the carrier of magnetic particles exhibited highly detection sensitivity for influenza virus A with LOD values of 7.35 pg/mL and 80.92 pg/mL by addressing the photoluminescence and CD spectra of QDs in the supernatant, respectively. However, for in vivo or intracellular sensing, the challenge for fluorescence imaging is developing fluorimetric nanoprobes that enable visualization with low background autofluorescence and good tissue penetrability (Lu et al., 2020). Meanwhile, metal nanomaterial-mediated assemblies can give rise to intense CD at visible or near infrared wavelengths, because of their scale and strong electron polarizability of inorganic compounds (Kuzyk et al., 2012). In this regard, Xu's group fabricated Au–UCNP pyramids via DNA driven self-assembly of AuNPs and UCNPs (Li et al., 2016), which displayed strong plasmonic CD at 521 nm and quenched luminescence, for real-time sensing of intracellular microRNA (miRNA) (Fig. 5 A). The Au–UCNP pyramids would undergo disassembly in the presence of target miRNA, leading to a decrease in CD signal and restoration of luminescence in 500–600 nm. Hence, the target miRNA can be monitored by CD intensity ranging from 0.073 to 43.65 fmol/10 μgRNA with a LOD of 0.03 fmol/10 μgRNA, whereas by the fluorescent intensity ranging from 0.16 to 43.65 fmol/10 μgRNA with a LOD of 0.12 fmol/10 μgRNA. Later, they also developed a sensor of AuNR-UCNP tetramers via DNA-driven self-assembly strategy (Wu et al., 2016). The propeller-like geometry of tetramers gave rise to strong chiroptical activity. By tuning the gap between the centrally located UCNP and AuNRs, a plasmon enhanced upconversion luminescence, up to 21.3-fold of that of original UCNPs, was achieved in the tetramers. Target oligonucleotides would enlarge the gap between central UCNP and AuNRs, leading to a decrease in the CD and luminescence. Hence, the strong chiroptical and luminescent activity of the tetramers enabled dual-modal biosensing of oligonucleotides with attomole (106 copies) sensitivity, as well as enhanced reliability and versatility.

In recent years, ICP-MS, a popular analytical technique for metal elements or isotopes, has gained increasing attention in environmental and biological research because of its ultrahigh sensitivity, accurate quantitation ability, ultrawide dynamic range, and long-term signal stability (Corte Rodríguez et al., 2017). The state-of-the-art fluorescent materials such as metal–organic frameworks, QDs, noble metal nanoclusters, and UCNPs often intrinsically contain large number of metal stable isotopes, which can be efficiently quantified by ICP-MS. For example, Hu et al. established a dual-modal sensing method for sensitive in-field fluorescence detection and accurate laboratory mass spectrometric quantification of a typical explosive such as trinitrotoluene (TNT) (Hu et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 5B, the TNT aptamer was combined with a poly thymine (poly-T) sequence in one single-stranded DNA from the 3′–5′ terminal. The resultant ssDNA could be hydrolyzed into single nucleotides from 3′ to 5′ catalyzed by Exo I, whereas the presence of TNT would block hydrolysis, thereby preserving the completeness of DNA. Subsequently, in situ synthesis of copper nanocluster along the preserved template poly-T sequence was conducted. Hence, adopting the resultant copper nanoclusters as a signal generator, the target TNT could be easily analyzed by a fluorimeter with a LOD of 0.17 ng/mL and accurately and stably analyzed by ICP-MS with a LOD of 0.32 pg/mL.

Recently, fluorimetric-ECL dual-modal sensors have been developed (Jiang et al., 2017). ECL is free of the interference of charging/discharging current, side reactions of conventional CL, and background emissions from unselective photoexcitation. rGO-based nanomaterials, which can be used as excellent fluorescence and ECL probes, have drawn increasing interest. For example, Wang et al. developed nitrogen-doped GQD-based immunoassay for OTA detection, and a nanosensor of graphene oxide loaded with CdTe QDs/AuNPs for amoxicillin detection (Wang et al., 2015).

Considering that catalytic reactions could generate insoluble gas and regulate fluorescence output, simple, rapid, cost-effective, and portable fluorimetric-pressure dual-modal sensors can be established. For example, Wang and co-authors prepared a CuO–NiO/C heterojunction nanofiber via electrospinning and established a dual-modal sensor for the detection of cancer cells after tagging folic acid onto the resultant fiber (Fig. 5C). The nanofiber exhibited high catalytic activity to catalyze the reduction of rhodamine 6G and hydrolysis of ammonia borane for generating H2, thereby outputting fluorescence variation and gas pressure increase (Wang et al., 2018d).

Cross-linked polymer hydrogel networks, which can respond to external stimuli through volume changes, are potential smart materials for developing simple POC sensors. Moreover, by incorporating functional NPs (e.g., QDs and AuNPs) or molecules (e.g., enzymes, organic dyes, and aptamers) with smart hydrogel, smart hybrid materials with designed properties (e.g., fluorescence, plasmon, and catalytic or selective recognition) can open wide venue for analytical applications. Park and co-workers fabricated a glucose nanosensor by immobilizing GOx, HRP, and C-dots within the porous poly (acrylic acid) hydrogel microdroplets (Park and Park, 2018). The sensing mechanism is shown in Fig. 5D, and gluconic acid and •OH radicals are produced from the cascade enzymatic reaction involving GOx, HRP, and glucose; the •OH radicals can efficiently quench the luminescence of C-dots, whereas the gluconic acid decreases the local pH, leading to evident size reduction of the hydrogel microdroplets.

Given the rapid development of material chemistry and nanotechnology, the dual-modal sensing techniques have achieved the enormous strides in recent years. Among various signal transducers, the colorimetric/fluorimetric signal channel with simple signal readout and detection protocol has laid the foundation for the development of dual-modal sensors. In addition, the integration of the colorimetric/fluorimetric signal with other signal outputs has greatly broaden the applications of dual-modal sensors because of its synergistic effect on the sensing outcome. Although the colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensing technologies promote the application flexibility; improve analytical performances, including enlarged dynamic linear range and enhanced accuracy, a few dual-modal sensors have been approved for practical uses. Therefore, many challenges still need to be addressed to boost their practical applications.
(1)
The advantages of dual-modal sensing primarily come from their ability to simultaneously use two different signals to indicate the concentration of the target analytes. In addition, dual-modal sensors commonly require two different devices for collecting detection signals, which also increase the difficulty in practical applications. Thus, developing some integrated signal readers, particularly portable devices, is necessary to meet the requirements of POCT detection. Moreover, the integration of dual-modal sensors with paper-based strip, microfluidic chip, or intelligent wearable equipment can promote its applications in the POCT field.
(2)
As previously discussed, the fabrication of dual-modal sensors is dependent on the preparation of dual-functional detection probes. The dual-functional detection probes are mostly prepared by integrating two different signal units into one sensing platform. However, the complex synthesis of signal units and inflexible integrations could result in poor robustness and reproducibility from batch to batch. Hence, developing techniques with fine controllability in synthesis, functional modification, and assembly of certain units is important in preparing dual-functional nanoprobes in the future.
(3)
With the development of analytical chemistry, some emerging signal transductions with specific properties for over-increasing demands have been proposed, such as photoacoustic signal (Repenko et al., 2018), NIR-II fluorescence (Sheng et al., 2018), afterglow luminescence (Ni et al., 2018) and dark-field light scatters (Liang et al., 2017). These signal transductions could be used to extend the applications of dual-modal sensing technologies from in vitro to in vivo diagnosis. On the contrary, some frontier biotechnologies, including CRISPR (Xiong et al., 2019) and DNA origami (Castro et al., 2011) have attracted considerable attention in bioanalytical applications because they can remarkablely improve the sensitivity, specificity, and properties of spatiotemporal detection. Therefore, the use of burgeoning materials capable of outputting the above-mentioned emerging types of signals and frontier biotechnologies would facilitate dual-modal sensors with superior analytical performances.
(4)
Multiplex detection methods compared with conventional single-target-analyte detection methods have accurate, reliable detection/diagnosis results under certain cases because they can provide more sample information (Wu et al., 2020b). Among the existing colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensors, only few have multiplex detection capabilities. Thus, dual-functional probes that can be applied in the existing multiplex detection platforms (e.g., multiplex ICTS, microarray, and suspension array) with good compatibility are favorable.

We believe that the colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensing is a suitable strategy to meet the ever-increasing demands and challenges in practice, inspired by the manifested promotion in analytical efficiency and performance. We also believe that this field could be improved with the acceleration effect of the collective toolbox of material chemistry and biotechnology. In this review, we try to provide an important guideline for the rational design of colorimetric/fluorimetric dual-modal sensors, which is also conductive to develop the multi-modal sensors integrated with three or more signals. This review could attract widespread interest of numerous fields such as material chemistry, nanotechnology, clinical diagnosis, food safety, and environmental monitoring.
